Section 8 develops one application in detail: using the Low-Low LISA designation as a data-driven, regionally coherent screen for "persistent recovery deficit" areas under the 2023 CRA modernization framework, illustrated with a back-of-envelope calculation of the housing-wealth shortfall in a hypothetical assessment area. The CDBG formula and FEMA BRIC prioritization are noted as secondary applications of the same descriptive screen. In each case, the recovery index and the LISA designation function as targeting criteria, reproducible from public data, not as causal claims about the effect of the programs themselves.

Several limitations deserve acknowledgment. In the descriptive analysis, the self-reported nature of VALUEH introduces measurement error, though @goodman1992 find no systematic income-related bias, and the PUMA boundary change between 2000 and 2010 vintages introduces geographic approximation in the pre-2012 data. The county-level causal design addresses both concerns directly, by using the transaction-based FHFA index and geographically stable counties, but it carries its own limitations. The exclusion restrictions cannot be tested directly; the falsification test identifies the Bartik instrument as invalid and the over-identification test is borderline (Sargan $p = 0.066$), and the disagreement between the two instruments means at least one channel-specific exclusion restriction is failing. The non-identification result is therefore best read as the absence of any instrument here that is both strong and valid, rather than as proof that recession severity is causally inert through every conceivable channel. The strong spatial dependence in the outcome (Moran's I = 0.70) is the binding constraint on inference: it is what turns the marginally significant heteroskedasticity-robust estimate into an insignificant one under clustering and Conley corrections, and any future county-level design will face the same constraint. Future work that can access within-household or within-property panel data tracking the same units across both shocks, or that brings additional independent instruments to bear, could sharpen the channel decomposition; but the spatial-inequality findings, the recovery index, the clustering results, and the compound-disadvantage targeting tool, are the paper's firmest contributions and do not depend on resolving the causal question.

# Data and Code Availability {.unnumbered}

Data and replication materials are available at: [Harvard Dataverse](https://doi.org/10.7910/DVN/P4R9RQ).

\newpage

# Declarations {.unnumbered}

**Competing interests:** The author declares no competing interests.

**Funding:** The author received no specific funding for this research.

**AI usage:** Some sections of this paper were copy-edited using Perplexity AI (Model: Sonar) to improve language clarity, grammar and structure. The research design, all empirical analyses, data collection, interpretation of results and content are entirely my own. The AI tool did not generate research ideas, conduct analyses or produce the underlying data or code.

\newpage

# References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::

\newpage

# Appendix A: Variable Definitions and Sample Construction {.unnumbered}

::: {.content-visible when-format="pdf"}
```{=latex}
\begin{table}[htbp]
\centering
\caption*{Table A1: Variable Definitions and Data Sources}
\label{tbl:vars}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.3}
\small
\begin{tabular}{p{2.6cm}p{4.6cm}p{8.0cm}}
\toprule
Variable & Source and Archive Path & Definition and Construction \\
\midrule
\texttt{VALUEH} & IPUMS ACS \newline \texttt{raw/ipums\_acs/}\newline\texttt{usa\_00010.csv,}\newline\texttt{usa\_00011.csv} & Owner-reported market value. Codes 0, 9,999,998, and 9,999,999 excluded. Winsorized at 1st/99th percentiles within PUMA-year cells. \\
\texttt{HHINCOME} & IPUMS ACS \newline \texttt{raw/ipums\_acs/}\newline\texttt{usa\_00010.csv} & Total household income. Code 9,999,999 excluded. Used for state-specific, year-specific income quintiles. \\
\texttt{HHWT} & IPUMS ACS \newline \texttt{raw/ipums\_acs/}\newline\texttt{usa\_00010.csv,}\newline\texttt{usa\_00011.csv} & Household survey weight. All means and quantiles are HHWT-weighted. \\
\texttt{puma\_geoid} & Constructed & 2-digit state FIPS + 5-digit PUMA code; matches TIGER GEOID10. \\
\texttt{recovery\_index} & Constructed \newline \texttt{processed/}\newline\texttt{puma\_recovery\_}\newline\texttt{index.csv} & 2021 median home value divided by (2012 median home value times CPI factor 1.180). CPI factor from \texttt{raw/cpi\_u/cpi\_u\_annual.csv}. \\
\texttt{risk\_score} & FEMA NRI v1.20 \newline \texttt{raw/fema\_nri/}\newline\texttt{NRI\_Table\_}\newline\texttt{CensusTracts.csv} & Population-weighted mean RISK\_SCORE within PUMA. Crosswalk via Census Gazetteer. Output at \texttt{processed/nri\_puma.csv}. \\
\texttt{sovi\_score} & FEMA NRI v1.20 \newline \texttt{raw/fema\_nri/}\newline\texttt{NRI\_Table\_}\newline\texttt{CensusTracts.csv} & Population-weighted mean SOVI\_SCORE (Social Vulnerability). \\
\texttt{resl\_score} & FEMA NRI v1.20 \newline \texttt{raw/fema\_nri/}\newline\texttt{NRI\_Table\_}\newline\texttt{CensusTracts.csv} & Population-weighted mean RESL\_SCORE (Community Resilience). \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}

\vspace{4pt}
\footnotesize All archive paths are relative to the companion \texttt{data and Replication codes/} folder.
\end{table}
```
:::

::: {.content-visible when-format="docx"}
**Table A1: Variable Definitions and Data Sources**

| Variable | Source | Archive Path | Definition |
|:---------|:-------|:------------|:-----------|
| VALUEH | IPUMS ACS | raw/ipums_acs/usa_00010.csv; usa_00011.csv | Owner-reported home value; codes 0, 9,999,998, 9,999,999 excluded |
| HHINCOME | IPUMS ACS | raw/ipums_acs/usa_00010.csv | Total household income; code 9,999,999 excluded |
| HHWT | IPUMS ACS | raw/ipums_acs/usa_00010.csv; usa_00011.csv | Household survey weight; all weighted statistics |
| puma_geoid | Constructed | -- | 2-digit state FIPS + 5-digit PUMA code |
| recovery_index | Constructed | processed/puma_recovery_index.csv | 2021 median / (2012 median × 1.180); CPI from raw/cpi_u/ |
| risk_score | FEMA NRI v1.20 | raw/fema_nri/NRI_Table_CensusTracts.csv | Population-weighted mean RISK_SCORE within PUMA |
| sovi_score | FEMA NRI v1.20 | raw/fema_nri/NRI_Table_CensusTracts.csv | Population-weighted mean SOVI_SCORE within PUMA |
| resl_score | FEMA NRI v1.20 | raw/fema_nri/NRI_Table_CensusTracts.csv | Population-weighted mean RESL_SCORE within PUMA |

*All paths are relative to the companion `data and Replication codes/` folder.*
:::

**Sample restrictions.** The ACS sample is restricted to: (1) household records only (GQ codes 1 and 2, excluding group quarters); (2) owner-occupied units (OWNERSHP = 1); (3) households with valid reported income (HHINCOME $\neq$ 9,999,999); and (4) units with valid reported home values (VALUEH $\notin \{0, 9{,}999{,}998, 9{,}999{,}999\}$). After these restrictions, the 2007--2021 dataset contains approximately 8.96 million household-year observations. The 2012--2021 spatial analysis uses a separate IPUMS extract with 1,769,702 households.

**PUMA vintage alignment.** The 2007 and 2009 ACS use 2000-vintage PUMA codes, while the 2010-vintage PUMA boundaries used in the TIGER shapefiles were introduced with the 2012 ACS. For the 2007--2021 nominal analysis, PUMAs with complete data across all four years are retained regardless of vintage; the PUMA identifier constructed from the IPUMS STATEFIP and PUMA codes matches the TIGER GEOID10 field when the same vintage is in use. Where 2000 and 2010 codes diverge, the geographic match is approximate. The 2012--2021 spatial analysis avoids this complication entirely because both years use 2010-vintage codes.

\newpage

# Appendix B: Spatial Autocorrelation Details {.unnumbered}

**Detailed description of the recovery surface (Figure 2).** The geographic pattern of the real recovery index is striking and immediately legible. A thick band of low recovery (recovery index below 1.1, shown in dark purple in Figure 2) runs from western Michigan eastward through northern Indiana and Ohio, with tendrils into central Illinois and a separate concentration in the lower Mississippi Delta and parts of the Appalachian coalfields. At the other extreme, the Mountain West and Sun Belt corridors from Phoenix through Denver to Salt Lake City, and from Atlanta through Tampa, show recovery indices well above 1.5.

**Detailed description of the LISA cluster map (Figure 4).** The Low-Low cores, in blue, form two geographically distinct structures. The larger is the Great Lakes industrial corridor: a contiguous bloc spanning the western lower peninsula of Michigan, northwestern Indiana, north-central Ohio, and stretching into western Pennsylvania and the southern tier of New York. The second is a less contiguous but geographically coherent set of markets running from southern Missouri through Arkansas into Louisiana and the Alabama Black Belt, tracking the Mississippi Delta's trajectory of economic decline. Scattered Low-Low clusters also appear in parts of West Virginia, eastern Kentucky, and isolated rural markets in the northern Plains. The High-High cores, in red, align with the Mountain West growth corridor (Phoenix, Denver, Boise, Salt Lake City) and with the Sun Belt from Atlanta through Tampa.

**Weight matrix construction.** Queen contiguity weights are computed on the CONUS PUMA polygon layer projected to Albers Equal Area (EPSG:5070) for topological consistency. Alaska (FIPS 02), Hawaii (FIPS 15), and Puerto Rico (FIPS 72) are excluded because their non-contiguous geometry would create isolated nodes with no queen neighbors. The weight matrix is row-standardized (style = "W"). Twenty-three island or enclave PUMAs with no queen neighbors receive a zero row and are handled with zero.policy = TRUE in all spatial computations.

**LISA significance.** Conditional randomization with 999 permutations is used to evaluate significance of local Moran's I statistics. PUMAs are assigned to the High-High quadrant if $z_i > 0$ and $\text{lag}(RI)_i > \bar{RI}$ and their local p-value is below 0.05; they are assigned to Low-Low if $z_i < 0$ and $\text{lag}(RI)_i < \bar{RI}$ and p < 0.05. The remaining significant PUMAs are spatial outliers (High-Low or Low-High). Not-significant PUMAs account for 1,553 of 2,336 classified CONUS PUMAs (66.5 percent).

**Spatial lag vs.\ spatial error.** A Lagrange Multiplier test is computed on the OLS residuals to choose between the spatial lag and spatial error specifications. The LM-lag statistic exceeds LM-error at conventional significance levels, consistent with the substantive argument that recovery spillovers across neighbors represent genuine economic interactions rather than shared but unobserved common shocks. The robustness-to-outlier check using Driscoll-Kraay standard errors on the OLS specification yields somewhat wider intervals but does not alter the sign or general significance of the key coefficients.

\newpage

# Appendix C: Income Group Recovery (Supplemental) {.unnumbered}

Figure A1 shows the state-level recovery rates by income group as described in Section 6.5. The near-uniform high recovery rates in nominal terms (96--100 percent across all groups) reflect the length of the study period: 14 years of generally rising nominal prices eventually lifted most state-income cells above their 2007 starting points. The policy-relevant finding is the catch-up regression in Table 5, which shows that the relationship between recession severity and COVID-era compensation is essentially absent for low-income homeowners.

![Income Group Recovery Rates: Share of State-Income-Group Cells Recovering to 2007 Nominal Values by 2021. Error bars show 95 percent confidence intervals. Income groups are defined using state-specific, year-specific quintile thresholds.](figures/figa1_income_recovery.png){#fig-incomerecov width=75%}

\newpage

# Appendix D: Robustness Checks and Cluster Cross-Tabulation (Supplemental) {.unnumbered}

**Inference and the channel decomposition.** The county-level causal results in Section 7 are estimated in `16_causal_2sls_revamp.R` (combined two-instrument 2SLS) and `17_causal_channels.R` (the per-instrument just-identified estimates); both sets of coefficients are reported together in Table 7, and the falsification and Rotemberg-weight diagnostics that invalidate the Bartik instrument are in Tables 8 and 9, generated by `19_iv_diagnostics.R`. The full inference battery, classical, heteroskedasticity-robust, state-clustered, and Conley spatial-HAC standard errors, is reported in both tables. The decisive pattern is that every apparently significant estimate (the OLS catch-up, the combined 2SLS under HC errors, and the HMDA-only credit-channel estimate) loses significance once the spatial dependence in the outcome is honored, while the strong instruments rule out a weak-identification explanation. Two extensions suggested during review, a within-property repeat-sales panel linking the same units across both shocks and additional independent instruments for the credit and labor channels, require data not present in the current replication archive and are noted as directions for future work.

**Overvaluation and the recovery typology.** Table D1 reports, as descriptive context, the joint distribution of k-means recovery-typology cluster membership and the 2007 price-to-income (P/I) ratio quartile, for the 1,068 PUMAs with both a valid P/I ratio and a cluster assignment. It shows that pre-crisis overvaluation and chronic non-recovery are largely separate phenomena: the formerly overvalued, top-P/I-quartile markets are concentrated in the Below-Average and Above-Average/Boom clusters, not in the Persistently Depressed cluster. This is descriptive only and is not used for causal inference; the P/I-ratio instrument it derives from is the weaker design documented in Appendix E.

::: {.content-visible when-format="pdf"}
```{=latex}
\begin{table}[htbp]
\centering
\caption*{Table D1: K-Means Cluster Membership by 2007 Price-to-Income Ratio Quartile}
\label{tbl:overvalued}
\small
\begin{tabular}{lcc}
\toprule
K-Means Cluster & Bottom 3 quartiles of P/I ($N=801$) & Top quartile of P/I ($N=267$) \\
\midrule
Persistently Depressed  & 541 (67.5\%) & 42 (15.7\%) \\
Below-Average Recovery  & 186 (23.2\%) & 114 (42.7\%) \\
Above-Average Recovery  & 4 (0.5\%)    & 66 (24.7\%) \\
Boom Markets            & 70 (8.7\%)   & 45 (16.9\%) \\
\bottomrule
\multicolumn{3}{l}{\footnotesize \textit{Note:} Cell entries are PUMA counts with column percentages in parentheses.} \\
\multicolumn{3}{l}{\footnotesize Top-quartile P/I PUMAs are concentrated in the Below-Average Recovery and} \\
\multicolumn{3}{l}{\footnotesize Above-Average/Boom clusters, not the Persistently Depressed cluster.} \\
\end{tabular}
\end{table}
```
:::

::: {.content-visible when-format="docx"}
**Table D1: K-Means Cluster Membership by 2007 Price-to-Income Ratio Quartile**

| K-Means Cluster | Bottom 3 quartiles of P/I (N=801) | Top quartile of P/I (N=267) |
|:-----|------:|------:|
| Persistently Depressed | 541 (67.5%) | 42 (15.7%) |
| Below-Average Recovery | 186 (23.2%) | 114 (42.7%) |
| Above-Average Recovery | 4 (0.5%) | 66 (24.7%) |
| Boom Markets | 70 (8.7%) | 45 (16.9%) |

*Cell entries are PUMA counts with column percentages in parentheses.*
:::

\newpage

# Appendix E: Superseded and Weaker Identification Strategies {.unnumbered}

This appendix documents two earlier and weaker instruments that the county-level design of Section 7 supersedes. They are reported in full so readers can see exactly why the headline causal estimate moved, and why the striking positive "sign reversal" of an earlier draft does not survive a credible design.

## E.1 PUMA Price-to-Income Instrument (Superseded) {.unnumbered}

An earlier version of this paper identified recession severity at the PUMA level by instrumenting the 2007--2009 percent change in home value with the 2007 local price-to-income (P/I) ratio, a proxy for pre-crisis overvaluation, controlling for the log 2007 home value, for 1,076 PUMAs with complete data. The first stage clears the classical relevance threshold (F = 15.1, or 13.8 without controls) with the expected negative sign (FS coefficient $-1.79$), but this relevance is fragile: the first-stage F falls to about 6.0 under heteroskedasticity-robust errors and about 2.8 under state clustering, both below the conventional value of 10. Against an OLS slope of $-0.181$, the 2SLS point estimate is $+0.802$ without state fixed effects and $+0.561$ with them, a sign reversal. That reversal is in the point estimate only. It is significant under classical errors but not under heteroskedasticity-robust errors (HC1 SE = 0.562 and 0.467), and it is unstable across spatial variance estimators, significant under state clustering and a 500 km Conley cutoff but not a 200 km cutoff. It also fails two qualifying checks: dropping 125 major coastal and technology-hub metro PUMAs flips the 2SLS coefficient to $-0.504$ and the Wu-Hausman test no longer rejects OLS (p = 0.183), and a placebo using the 2012--2019 window yields $-2.150$, the same sign as OLS.

Three problems motivated abandoning this design in favor of the county-level analysis. First, the instrument is weak under any robust inference. Second, it shares the self-reported 2007 home value with the base of the treatment variable, so part of the first stage is mechanical mean reversion and self-report measurement error rather than a clean economic channel. Third, the 2000-to-2010 PUMA boundary revision injects geographic noise into the pre-2012 panel. The county-level Bartik-plus-HMDA design in Section 7 fixes all three: strong instruments (joint F = 22.7), instruments measured independently of the FHFA price index, and geographically stable counties. Reassuringly, the credible design returns a 2SLS estimate ($-0.233$) with the same sign as OLS, confirming that the positive reversal reported here was an artifact of the weak instrument rather than a feature of the data.

## E.2 State-Level FHFA Instrument (Weak Instrument, Suggestive Only) {.unnumbered}

The state-level income-group analysis uses the percentage change in the FHFA All-Transactions House Price Index from 2003Q4 to 2007Q4 as an instrument for recession severity, separately by income group. As Table E1 shows, this instrument is weak: first-stage F-statistics of 4.1, 3.8, and 0.1 for the low-, middle-, and high-income groups fall well below the conventional relevance threshold of 10 (@staiger1997). The corresponding 2SLS estimates in Table E2 have wide confidence intervals and unstable signs across income groups, the signature of a weak-instrument problem rather than genuine heterogeneity in the treatment effect. These estimates are reported for transparency only and should not be used for causal inference. Future work with a stronger state-level instrument, such as the pre-crisis share of adjustable-rate mortgages from HMDA data, could improve precision at this level of aggregation.

::: {.content-visible when-format="pdf"}
```{=latex}
\begin{table}[htbp]
\centering
\caption*{Table E1: First-Stage Diagnostics, State-Level FHFA Instrument}
\label{tbl:firststage_state}
\small
\begin{tabular}{lcccccc}
\toprule
Income Group & Instrument & $N$ & FS Coeff. & FS SE & F-Stat & $R^2$ \\
\midrule
Low income    & FHFA HPI apprec.\ 2003Q4--2007Q4 & 51 & $-$0.151 & 0.074 & 4.1 & 0.078 \\
Middle income & FHFA HPI apprec.\ 2003Q4--2007Q4 & 51 & $-$0.133 & 0.068 & 3.8 & 0.072 \\
High income   & FHFA HPI apprec.\ 2003Q4--2007Q4 & 51 & $-$0.028 & 0.080 & 0.1 & 0.002 \\
\bottomrule
\multicolumn{7}{l}{\footnotesize \textit{Note:} First stage: 2007--2009 pct.\ change regressed on FHFA pre-crisis HPI appreciation.} \\
\multicolumn{7}{l}{\footnotesize Conventional weak-instrument threshold: F $>$ 10.} \\
\end{tabular}
\end{table}
```
```{=latex}
\begin{table}[htbp]
\centering
\caption*{Table E2: OLS and 2SLS Estimates, State-Level FHFA Instrument (Weak Instrument)}
\label{tbl:iv_state}
\small
\begin{tabular}{lcccccc}
\toprule
Income Group & $N$ & Instrument & OLS $\hat{\beta}$ & 2SLS $\hat{\beta}$ & 2SLS SE & Wu-Hausman $p$ \\
\midrule
Low income    & 51 & FHFA HPI & $-$0.022 & $-$0.594 & 0.924 & 0.454 \\
Middle income & 51 & FHFA HPI & $-$0.240 & $+$0.003 & 1.016 & 0.625 \\
High income   & 51 & FHFA HPI & $-$0.222 & $-$3.820 & 10.37 & 0.302 \\
\bottomrule
\multicolumn{7}{l}{\footnotesize \textit{Note:} Outcome = pct.\ change in state-income-group weighted mean home value, 2019--2021.} \\
\multicolumn{7}{l}{\footnotesize Endogenous variable = pct.\ change, 2007--2009. Instrument is weak (F $<$ 5 for all groups);} \\
\multicolumn{7}{l}{\footnotesize estimates are suggestive only and not used for causal inference.} \\
\end{tabular}
\end{table}
```
:::

::: {.content-visible when-format="docx"}
**Table E1: First-Stage Diagnostics, State-Level FHFA Instrument**

| Income Group | Instrument | N | FS Coeff. | FS SE | F-Stat | R-sq |
|:------|:-----------|--:|----------:|------:|-------:|-----:|
| Low income | FHFA HPI apprec. 2003Q4-2007Q4 | 51 | -0.151 | 0.074 | 4.1 | 0.078 |
| Middle income | FHFA HPI apprec. 2003Q4-2007Q4 | 51 | -0.133 | 0.068 | 3.8 | 0.072 |
| High income | FHFA HPI apprec. 2003Q4-2007Q4 | 51 | -0.028 | 0.080 | 0.1 | 0.002 |

*Conventional weak-instrument threshold: F > 10.*

**Table E2: OLS and 2SLS Estimates, State-Level FHFA Instrument (Weak Instrument)**

| Income Group | N | Instrument | OLS Beta | 2SLS Beta | 2SLS SE | WH p-val |
|:------------|--:|:----------|--------:|----------:|--------:|--------:|
| Low income | 51 | FHFA HPI | -0.022 | -0.594 | 0.924 | 0.454 |
| Middle income | 51 | FHFA HPI | -0.240 | +0.003 | 1.016 | 0.625 |
| High income | 51 | FHFA HPI | -0.222 | -3.820 | 10.37 | 0.302 |

*Instrument is weak (F < 5 for all groups); estimates are suggestive only and not used for causal inference.*
:::
